title: Restore without Rewriting History
summary: Undo an unstaged edit or unstage a file while keeping the committed history intact.

sketchBlocks.title: Using git restore for Working Tree Files
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Git provides a powerful command called `git restore` that allows you to revert changes in your working directory. This command is particularly useful when you want to discard changes in a file that you haven't staged yet. 

Worked example: Suppose you have a file `report.txt` that you've modified but now wish to revert to its last committed state. You can use the command `git restore report.txt` to discard the changes and restore the file to its committed version. This operation affects only the working tree and does not alter the staging area or commit history.

sketchBlocks.title: Using git restore --staged to Unstage Files
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
The `git restore --staged` command is used to unstage files that have been added to the staging area. This is useful when you realize that a file should not be included in the next commit.

Worked example: Imagine you have staged a file `draft.txt` using `git add draft.txt`, but you decide it should not be part of the next commit. You can run `git restore --staged draft.txt` to remove it from the staging area while keeping the changes in your working directory.

sketchBlocks.title: Deciding Between git restore and git restore --staged
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Choosing the correct restore operation depends on your current situation. If you need to discard changes in the working directory, use `git restore`. If you need to unstage a file but keep its changes, use `git restore --staged`.

Worked example: You have modified and staged `summary.txt`, but you want to keep the changes without including them in the next commit. Use `git restore --staged summary.txt` to unstage it.

sketchBlocks.title: Avoiding Common Mistakes with git restore
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
A common mistake when using `git restore` is misunderstanding its scope. Remember, `git restore` affects only the working directory, while `git restore --staged` affects the staging area.

Worked example: If you mistakenly use `git restore` on a staged file, it will not unstage it. Instead, use `git restore --staged` to correctly unstage the file while preserving its changes.

quizDraft.title: Purpose of git restore
quizDraft.prompt: What is the primary purpose of the `git restore` command?
options: To revert changes in the working directory
To commit changes to the repository
quizDraft.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

quizDraft.title: Effect of git restore --staged
quizDraft.prompt: What happens when you use `git restore --staged` on a file?
options: The file is removed from the staging area but changes are kept
The file is deleted from the repository
The file is committed to the repository
The file is reverted to the last committed state
quizDraft.hint: Consider what `git restore --staged` does to the staging area.

help.concept: The `git restore --staged` command is used to unstage files while keeping their changes.
help.hint_1: Think about how `git restore --staged` affects the staging area.
help.hint_2: `git restore --staged` removes the file from the staging area but keeps its changes.

quizDraft.title: Command to Unstage a File
quizDraft.prompt: Fill in the blank to unstage a file while keeping its changes: `git restore --[blank1] <filename>`
quizDraft.hint: The option to unstage a file is `--the missing term`.

help.concept: The `git restore --the missing term` command is used to unstage files while keeping their changes.
help.hint_1: The option to unstage a file is `--the missing term`.
help.hint_2: Use `--the missing term` to unstage a file.

quizDraft.title: Command to Revert Changes
quizDraft.prompt: Fill in the blank to revert changes in the working directory: `git [blank1] <filename>`
quizDraft.hint: The command to revert changes is `the missing term`.

help.concept: The `git the missing term` command is used to revert changes in the working directory.
help.hint_1: The command to revert changes is `the missing term`.
help.hint_2: Use `the missing term` to revert changes.

quizDraft.title: Revert Changes in Working Directory
quizDraft.prompt:
You have modified a file named `notes.txt` in your working directory but want to discard these changes. Use the appropriate Git command to revert `notes.txt` to its last committed state.

This practice opens in a fresh workspace. First type `bash .zoeskoul/setup.sh` and press Enter, then type each requested Git command and press Enter.
quizDraft.hint: Read the Git task "Revert Changes in Working Directory" and identify the repository state you need to create.

help.concept: This Git exercise checks the repository state produced by "Revert Changes in Working Directory".
help.hint_1: Use git status before and after each small change so you can see what moved between the working tree, staging area, and history.
help.hint_2: Match the requested file names, branch names, and commit purpose before clicking Check Answer.

starterFiles.content:
# First type: bash .zoeskoul/setup.sh
# Press Enter, then complete the requested Git task.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
git init -q -b main
git config user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git config user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
printf '.zoeskoul/
main.sh
' >> .git/info/exclude
printf 'Git practice file 1\n' > notes.txt
